Secondary structure.
questions.explain: Primary structure is the linear order of amino acids in the polypeptide backbone.
choice_explanations: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of one polypeptide.
Correct. Primary structure is the linear order of amino acids in the polypeptide backbone.
Quaternary structure refers to the association of multiple polypeptide subunits.
Secondary structure refers to local folding patterns such as alpha helices and beta sheets, not the amino acid order itself.

questions.text: A mutation changes one amino acid in the middle of a polypeptide. Which level of structure is altered first?
choices: Tertiary structure only.
Quaternary structure only.
Primary structure.
Secondary structure only.
questions.explain: Changing even one amino acid directly changes the sequence of the chain before any higher-level folding consequences are considered.
choice_explanations: Tertiary effects are possible, but the first direct change is still primary structure.
Quaternary structure involves multiple subunits and may not even apply to the protein in question.
Correct. Changing even one amino acid directly changes the sequence of the chain before any higher-level folding consequences are considered.
Secondary structure may change later, but the immediate direct change is to the amino acid sequence.

questions.text: What mainly stabilizes alpha helices and beta pleated sheets in proteins?
choices: Phosphodiester bonds between amino acid side chains.
Hydrogen bonds between parts of the polypeptide backbone.
Ionic bonds between glycerol and fatty acids.
Peptide bonds between different polypeptide chains.
questions.explain: Secondary structure is stabilized largely by hydrogen bonding patterns in the backbone, not by side-chain sequence alone.
choice_explanations: Phosphodiester bonds belong to nucleic acids, not typical protein folding.
Correct. Secondary structure is stabilized largely by hydrogen bonding patterns in the backbone, not by side-chain sequence alone.
That is unrelated lipid chemistry.
Peptide bonds form the backbone itself, not the secondary-structure interactions described here.

questions.text: Which level of protein structure refers to the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide chain?
choices: Tertiary structure.
Quaternary structure.
Primary structure.
Secondary structure.
questions.explain: Tertiary structure includes interactions among R groups that fold one chain into its final shape.
choice_explanations: Correct. Tertiary structure includes interactions among R groups that fold one chain into its final shape.
Quaternary structure involves more than one polypeptide subunit.
Primary structure is only the amino acid sequence.
Secondary structure is local folding pattern, not the full overall shape of one chain.

questions.text: A functional hemoglobin molecule contains multiple polypeptide chains associated together. This feature is an example of
choices: secondary structure.
tertiary structure only.
quaternary structure.
primary structure.
questions.explain: When several polypeptide subunits assemble into one functional protein complex, the protein has quaternary structure.
choice_explanations: Secondary structure refers to helices and sheets within chains.
Each subunit has tertiary structure, but the association among subunits is quaternary.
Correct. When several polypeptide subunits assemble into one functional protein complex, the protein has quaternary structure.
Primary structure concerns sequence within one chain.

questions.text: Why can extreme pH reduce enzyme activity without breaking every peptide bond in the protein?
choices: Changes in pH can disrupt ionic and hydrogen-bond interactions that maintain the protein's shape, altering the active site.
Extreme pH converts peptide bonds directly into phosphodiester bonds.
Extreme pH removes all amino acids from the protein one at a time.
Extreme pH makes the enzyme more hydrophobic, so substrate concentration becomes irrelevant.
questions.explain: Loss of function often comes from altered folding rather than cleavage of the backbone itself.
choice_explanations: Correct. Loss of function often comes from altered folding rather than cleavage of the backbone itself.
pH changes do not convert the protein backbone into a nucleic-acid-like linkage.
Denaturation usually changes shape before complete hydrolysis of the chain.
The key issue is disruption of structure, especially around the active site.

questions.text: A protein unfolds after heating. Which statement best explains the loss of function?
choices: Heating makes the protein more acidic, which guarantees higher catalytic rate.
Heating disrupts interactions that maintain the protein's shape, so the active site no longer has the correct structure.
Heating causes all covalent peptide bonds to break immediately, which is the only way activity can be lost.
Heating changes every amino acid into a sugar, so the protein is no longer a protein.
questions.explain: Protein function depends heavily on shape, and heat can denature the molecule without necessarily destroying the primary sequence.
choice_explanations: Heat often reduces function by disrupting structure rather than guaranteeing faster catalysis.
Correct. Protein function depends heavily on shape, and heat can denature the molecule without necessarily destroying the primary sequence.
Activity can be lost through denaturation even if most peptide bonds remain intact.
Heat does not convert amino acids into carbohydrates.

questions.text: Which interaction is most directly associated with stabilizing tertiary structure rather than primary structure?
choices: The covalent linkage of nucleotides in DNA.
The branching pattern of glycogen.
The order of amino acids along the polypeptide backbone.
Interactions among amino acid side chains such as ionic attractions, hydrogen bonds, and disulfide bridges.
questions.explain: Tertiary structure comes from side-chain interactions that fold the chain into its overall shape.
choice_explanations: That describes nucleic acid structure, not protein structure.
That is carbohydrate structure, not protein structure.
That is primary structure.
Correct. Tertiary structure comes from side-chain interactions that fold the chain into its overall shape.

questions.text: A protein contains two cysteine side chains that form a covalent link. Which feature is being described?
choices: A peptide bond that extends primary structure.
A hydrogen bond between complementary bases.
A glycosidic bond between glucose monomers.
A disulfide bridge that can help stabilize tertiary structure.
questions.explain: Disulfide bridges form between sulfhydryl groups and can strengthen the folded structure of a protein.
choice_explanations: Peptide bonds join amino acids in the backbone, not cysteine side chains in folded regions.
That describes a nucleic acid interaction.
That is carbohydrate chemistry.
Correct. Disulfide bridges form between sulfhydryl groups and can strengthen the folded structure of a protein.

questions.text: A student says, 'Secondary structure is the final three-dimensional shape of the whole protein.' What is the best evaluation?
choices: The statement is fully incorrect because secondary structure refers only to the amino acid sequence.

The statement is partly correct because secondary structure involves three-dimensional folding, but it is incorrect because it refers only to local patterns such as helices and sheets, not the full overall protein shape.
The statement is fully incorrect because proteins have only primary and quaternary structure.
The statement is fully correct because alpha helices always determine the entire protein shape by themselves.
questions.explain: This is a common partial-truth trap: secondary structure is indeed 3-D, but only at the local level.
choice_explanations: Sequence is primary structure, not secondary.
Correct. This is a common partial-truth trap: secondary structure is indeed 3-D, but only at the local level.
Proteins can have primary, secondary, tertiary, and sometimes quaternary structure.
A protein's full shape also depends on tertiary and sometimes quaternary structure.

questions.text: Which change would most directly alter a protein's primary structure?
choices: Changing the pH around a folded enzyme without changing its sequence.
Separating two subunits of a multimeric protein.
Disrupting hydrogen bonds in an alpha helix.
Replacing one amino acid in the polypeptide chain with a different amino acid.
questions.explain: Primary structure is the amino acid sequence, so any substitution in that sequence changes it directly.
choice_explanations: That may alter higher-order folding, but not the underlying amino acid order.
That changes quaternary structure.
That changes secondary structure more directly than primary structure.
Correct. Primary structure is the amino acid sequence, so any substitution in that sequence changes it directly.

questions.text: Why can proteins with different primary structures sometimes still have similar functions?
choices: Different primary structures are impossible; all proteins use the same amino acid order.
Function depends only on quaternary structure, so primary structure never matters.
Any protein with a different primary structure must become a lipid instead.
Different amino acid sequences can sometimes fold into active sites with similar shapes and chemical properties.
questions.explain: Function depends on final structure and chemistry, not only on sequence identity.
choice_explanations: Proteins vary widely in sequence.
Primary structure strongly influences folding and therefore function.
A different sequence does not convert the molecule into a different macromolecule class.
Correct. Function depends on final structure and chemistry, not only on sequence identity.

questions.text: Which level of structure is absent in a functional protein made of only one polypeptide chain?
choices: Secondary structure.
Tertiary structure.
Quaternary structure.
Primary structure.
questions.explain: A protein with only one polypeptide can still have primary, secondary, and tertiary structure but not quaternary structure.
choice_explanations: A single polypeptide can still form helices or sheets.
A single polypeptide can still fold into an overall three-dimensional shape.
Correct. A protein with only one polypeptide can still have primary, secondary, and tertiary structure but not quaternary structure.
Any polypeptide has an amino acid sequence.

questions.text: A change in which part of an amino acid would be most likely to alter the tertiary structure of a protein without changing the peptide backbone directly?
choices: The peptide bond oxygen in a different molecule class.
The shared amino group that is part of every amino acid's backbone.
The phosphate group attached to every amino acid.
The R group.
questions.explain: The side chain determines many of the interactions that stabilize tertiary structure.
choice_explanations: This is not a separate amino acid part being altered in the sense asked.
Backbone groups matter, but changes in side chains are the most direct way to alter R-group interactions without changing the peptide backbone itself.
Not every amino acid has a phosphate group.
Correct. The side chain determines many of the interactions that stabilize tertiary structure.

questions.text: A protein loses activity after its tertiary structure is disrupted, but sequencing shows the amino acid order is unchanged. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Only quaternary structure determines whether any protein can function.
Primary structure is irrelevant to protein function once the protein is made.
Proteins work the same after denaturation as long as peptide bonds are intact.
Protein function depends on correct folding, not just on the primary sequence existing somewhere in the molecule.
questions.explain: The amino acid sequence is necessary, but the active form of the protein also requires proper three-dimensional structure.
choice_explanations: Many proteins function without quaternary structure.
Primary structure still matters because it influences folding.
Denaturation often destroys function because shape is crucial.
Correct. The amino acid sequence is necessary, but the active form of the protein also requires proper three-dimensional structure.

questions.text: A mutation causes a nonpolar amino acid in the hydrophobic core is replaced by a charged amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The substitution cannot matter because all amino acids have the same side chain chemistry.
The new charged side chain may disrupt hydrophobic packing and destabilize the folded protein.
Only the quaternary structure of DNA is affected by amino acid substitutions.
The protein will become a carbohydrate because a charged residue was introduced.
questions.explain: The new charged side chain may disrupt hydrophobic packing and destabilize the folded protein.
choice_explanations: Amino acids differ precisely because their side chains have different chemistry, so replacing one type with another can matter a lot.
Correct. The new charged side chain may disrupt hydrophobic packing and destabilize the folded protein.
Amino acid substitutions affect proteins, not DNA quaternary structure.
Changing one amino acid does not transform the entire macromolecule class into a carbohydrate.

questions.text: A mutation causes a negatively charged amino acid on the protein surface is replaced by a positively charged amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The change can alter ionic interactions and potentially change how the protein interacts with other molecules.
The change affects only primary structure in theory and can never influence higher levels of structure.
The substitution has no effect because surface residues never influence protein behavior.
The new residue automatically creates a peptide bond with any nearby water molecule.
questions.explain: The change can alter ionic interactions and potentially change how the protein interacts with other molecules.
choice_explanations: Correct. The change can alter ionic interactions and potentially change how the protein interacts with other molecules.
A primary-structure change can absolutely influence higher-order structure and function.
Surface residues often matter because they participate in binding and folding interactions.
Water does not automatically form peptide bonds with amino acid side chains.

questions.text: A mutation causes a cysteine that normally forms a disulfide bridge is replaced by serine. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The protein may lose a stabilizing disulfide bridge and become less stable.
Disulfide bridges are features of carbohydrates, so the mutation should not matter much to a protein.
The substitution guarantees that the protein's primary structure becomes unchanged again after folding.
The protein will gain a new phosphodiester bond in the same location.
questions.explain: The protein may lose a stabilizing disulfide bridge and become less stable.
choice_explanations: Correct. The protein may lose a stabilizing disulfide bridge and become less stable.
Disulfide bridges are important in many proteins, not carbohydrates.
Folding does not erase the fact that the amino acid sequence changed.
Disulfide bridges are sulfur-based protein interactions, not phosphodiester bonds.

questions.text: A mutation causes glycine in a tight turn is replaced by a bulky amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Bulky residues always make proteins more stable because they add more mass.
The bulky side chain may interfere with local folding because glycine often fits where larger residues cannot.
The change would matter only if the amino acid were in DNA rather than in the protein.
The mutation cannot change shape because turns are determined only by the solvent, not the amino acid sequence.
questions.explain: The bulky side chain may interfere with local folding because glycine often fits where larger residues cannot.
choice_explanations: Greater mass does not automatically mean better stability; steric interference can destabilize the fold.
Correct. The bulky side chain may interfere with local folding because glycine often fits where larger residues cannot.
Amino acid identity matters in proteins themselves, not only in DNA.
Local folding depends strongly on which amino acids are present.

questions.text: A mutation causes a polar amino acid in an enzyme active site is replaced by a nonpolar amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Active sites are made of lipids, so amino acid substitutions there are irrelevant.
Only the protein's color can change, not its catalytic behavior.
Substrate binding or catalysis may change because the chemistry of the active site has changed.
The change affects only the DNA codon, not the protein's behavior after translation.
questions.explain: Substrate binding or catalysis may change because the chemistry of the active site has changed.
choice_explanations: Active sites of enzymes are built from amino acid side chains, not lipids.
Active-site chemistry is central to function, not just appearance.
Correct. Substrate binding or catalysis may change because the chemistry of the active site has changed.
Once the substitution appears in the protein, it can directly affect behavior.

questions.text: A mutation causes a hydrophilic surface amino acid is replaced by a hydrophobic amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Surface residues cannot affect tertiary structure because only buried residues matter.
The new residue may reduce favorable interaction with water and alter surface behavior or folding.
Hydrophobic residues on a surface always make the protein more soluble in water.
The substitution turns the amino acid into a monosaccharide.
questions.explain: The new residue may reduce favorable interaction with water and alter surface behavior or folding.
choice_explanations: Surface residues can influence folding, stability, and binding.
Correct. The new residue may reduce favorable interaction with water and alter surface behavior or folding.
Hydrophobic residues generally interact less favorably with water, not more.
An amino acid substitution does not convert the monomer into a sugar.

questions.text: A mutation causes a positively charged amino acid that binds DNA is replaced by a neutral amino acid. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The mutation changes the DNA into RNA, which is why binding changes.
Binding to the negatively charged DNA backbone may weaken.
Only membrane fluidity can be affected by losing a charged side chain.
DNA binding will strengthen because neutral residues attract phosphate groups more than charged residues do.
questions.explain: Binding to the negatively charged DNA backbone may weaken.
choice_explanations: Changing a protein residue does not convert DNA into RNA.
Correct. Binding to the negatively charged DNA backbone may weaken.
The most direct effect is on DNA-protein interaction, not membrane fluidity.
The phosphate backbone is negatively charged, so removing positive charge can weaken the interaction.

questions.text: A mutation causes an amino acid substitution introduces proline into the middle of an alpha helix. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The helix may be disrupted because proline often introduces a bend that is unfavorable in helices.
Proline can affect only quaternary structure, never secondary structure.
Alpha helices are made entirely of glycosidic bonds, so amino acid identity is irrelevant.
The helix becomes stronger because proline forms extra phosphodiester bonds.
questions.explain: The helix may be disrupted because proline often introduces a bend that is unfavorable in helices.
choice_explanations: Correct. The helix may be disrupted because proline often introduces a bend that is unfavorable in helices.
Introducing proline can directly affect secondary structure.
Alpha helices are protein secondary structures, not carbohydrate structures.
Helices are stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds, not phosphodiester bonds.

questions.text: A mutation causes a mutation swaps one acidic residue for another acidic residue in a region not involved in binding. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The effect is impossible because acidic residues cannot replace one another in proteins.
The effect must be catastrophic because every amino acid substitution breaks all tertiary structure.
The protein automatically becomes a lipid because the sequence changed.
The effect may be small because the replacement preserves similar side-chain chemistry.
questions.explain: The effect may be small because the replacement preserves similar side-chain chemistry.
choice_explanations: Amino acid substitutions among residues of similar properties are possible and common.
Not every substitution is catastrophic; similar chemistry can preserve function.
Sequence changes do not transform proteins into lipids.
Correct. The effect may be small because the replacement preserves similar side-chain chemistry.

questions.text: A mutation causes an amino acid with a sulfhydryl group is replaced by one lacking sulfur. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Sulfhydryl groups are found only in nucleotides, not amino acids.
Any disulfide-based stabilization involving that residue could be lost.
The substitution changes the peptide backbone into a phospholipid tail.
The protein gains extra sulfur-hydrogen bonds that always increase activity.
questions.explain: Any disulfide-based stabilization involving that residue could be lost.
choice_explanations: Cysteine side chains contain sulfhydryl groups in proteins.
Correct. Any disulfide-based stabilization involving that residue could be lost.
The peptide backbone remains a peptide backbone.
Losing sulfur usually removes the possibility of that particular disulfide linkage rather than adding more sulfur-based bonds.

questions.text: A mutation causes a small neutral side chain in a tightly packed region is replaced by a much larger hydrophobic side chain. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: No effect is possible because hydrophobic residues are always interchangeable.
The substitution matters only to primary structure and cannot influence the final shape.
Steric clashes may distort local packing even though both residues are nonpolar.
Larger side chains automatically create more hydrogen bonds and guarantee stability.
questions.explain: Steric clashes may distort local packing even though both residues are nonpolar.
choice_explanations: Hydrophobic residues can still differ in size and shape, which matters in tight packing.
Primary-structure changes can propagate to final folding.
Correct. Steric clashes may distort local packing even though both residues are nonpolar.
Large hydrophobic side chains do not automatically form more hydrogen bonds.

questions.text: A mutation causes a residue involved in an ionic attraction is replaced by an uncharged residue. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Only carbohydrates use ionic attractions; proteins do not.
The ionic interaction may be lost, potentially altering folding or binding.
The ionic interaction becomes a peptide bond, so the structure is unchanged.
An uncharged side chain always stabilizes a salt bridge better than a charged one.
questions.explain: The ionic interaction may be lost, potentially altering folding or binding.
choice_explanations: Proteins can contain important ionic attractions among side chains.
Correct. The ionic interaction may be lost, potentially altering folding or binding.
Peptide bonds are backbone linkages, not replacements for a lost side-chain attraction.
Salt bridges specifically depend on charged groups.

questions.text: A mutation causes an enzyme active-site histidine that can accept or donate H+ is replaced by leucine. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Leucine has the same side-chain chemistry as histidine, so activity should be unchanged.
The only effect is that the enzyme becomes a nucleic acid.
Catalysis may decrease because leucine cannot perform the same acid-base chemistry.
Catalysis will improve because any hydrophobic residue speeds proton transfer.
questions.explain: Catalysis may decrease because leucine cannot perform the same acid-base chemistry.
choice_explanations: Histidine and leucine differ strongly in side-chain chemistry.
Changing one residue does not make the molecule a nucleic acid.
Correct. Catalysis may decrease because leucine cannot perform the same acid-base chemistry.
Hydrophobic residues do not generally substitute for acid-base catalytic roles.

questions.text: A mutation causes an exposed charged amino acid is replaced by another charged amino acid of opposite sign. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Electrostatic interactions on the surface may change direction or strength.
The protein loses all peptide bonds immediately after the substitution.
The change is irrelevant because opposite charges are chemically identical.
Only phospholipid bilayers respond to charge reversal.
questions.explain: Electrostatic interactions on the surface may change direction or strength.
choice_explanations: Correct. Electrostatic interactions on the surface may change direction or strength.
A substitution does not erase the backbone peptide bonds.
Opposite charges are not chemically identical and can alter interactions.
Proteins are also sensitive to charge changes, not just membranes.

questions.text: A mutation causes a mutation changes one amino acid, but the protein still works normally. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The substituted residue may not be critical, or the new amino acid may have similar chemical properties.
The result proves that no mutation actually occurred in the protein-coding sequence.
The unchanged function means the protein must have turned into a carbohydrate temporarily.
The result proves that amino acid sequence never matters for proteins.
questions.explain: The substituted residue may not be critical, or the new amino acid may have similar chemical properties.
choice_explanations: Correct. The substituted residue may not be critical, or the new amino acid may have similar chemical properties.
A coding change can occur without abolishing function.
Stable protein function does not imply conversion into another macromolecule class.
One tolerated mutation does not mean sequence never matters.

questions.text: Which bond links one nucleotide to the next within a single nucleic acid strand?
choices: A glycosidic bond between glucose monomers.
A hydrogen bond.
A peptide bond.
A phosphodiester bond.
questions.explain: The sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA or RNA is held together by phosphodiester linkages.
choice_explanations: That describes carbohydrate polymers, not nucleic acid strands.
Hydrogen bonds commonly occur between complementary bases, not along the backbone of one strand.
Peptide bonds join amino acids, not nucleotides.
Correct. The sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA or RNA is held together by phosphodiester linkages.

questions.text: Which interaction helps hold two complementary DNA strands together?
choices: Ionic bonds between deoxyribose sugars.
Disulfide bridges between phosphate groups.
Peptide bonds between adjacent nucleotides on opposite strands.
Hydrogen bonds between complementary nitrogenous bases.
questions.explain: Base pairing depends on hydrogen bonding, whereas the backbone of each strand is covalent.
choice_explanations: The major specific interaction between strands is hydrogen bonding between bases.
Disulfide bridges involve sulfhydryl groups, not phosphate groups.
Peptide bonds do not connect nucleotides.
Correct. Base pairing depends on hydrogen bonding, whereas the backbone of each strand is covalent.

questions.text: What three components make up a nucleotide?
choices: A glycerol, three fatty acids, and a phosphate group.
A phosphate group, a five-carbon sugar, and a nitrogenous base.
An amino group, a carboxyl group, and an R group.
Two monosaccharides and a glycosidic bond.
questions.explain: Those three parts define the monomer of nucleic acids.
choice_explanations: That describes lipid-related components, not a nucleotide.
Correct. Those three parts define the monomer of nucleic acids.
That describes an amino acid.
That describes a disaccharide.

questions.text: Which statement best distinguishes DNA from a protein?
choices: DNA is a polymer of nucleotides, whereas a protein is a polymer of amino acids.
DNA and proteins are both built from glucose monomers but fold differently.
DNA is a polymer of amino acids, whereas a protein is a polymer of nucleotides.
DNA differs from proteins only because DNA contains more water molecules.
questions.explain: The two macromolecules differ in both monomers and typical linkage chemistry.
choice_explanations: Correct. The two macromolecules differ in both monomers and typical linkage chemistry.
Neither DNA nor proteins are glucose polymers.
This reverses the monomers.
The key difference is monomer type and structure, not water content.

questions.text: A researcher hydrolyzes an RNA molecule completely. Which monomers are produced?
choices: Amino acids.
Monosaccharides.
Glycerol and fatty acids.
Nucleotides.
questions.explain: Hydrolysis of a nucleic acid breaks the polymer into its nucleotide subunits.
choice_explanations: Those are the monomers of proteins.
Those are carbohydrate monomers.
Those are common lipid components.
Correct. Hydrolysis of a nucleic acid breaks the polymer into its nucleotide subunits.

questions.text: Why is a phosphodiester bond classified as covalent?
choices: It is weak and temporary like a hydrogen bond.
It forms only when complete electron transfer occurs between phosphate groups.
It forms only between separate DNA molecules and never within one molecule.
It involves the sharing of electrons between atoms in the nucleotide backbone.
questions.explain: Covalent bonds are defined by shared electrons, unlike hydrogen bonds or ionic attractions.
choice_explanations: Phosphodiester bonds are stable covalent bonds, not weak temporary interactions.
Complete electron transfer would describe ionic bonding, not the backbone linkage here.
Phosphodiester bonds are fundamental within a single nucleic acid strand.
Correct. Covalent bonds are defined by shared electrons, unlike hydrogen bonds or ionic attractions.

questions.text: Which statement is true about the sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA?
choices: It is held together mainly by hydrogen bonds between adjacent sugars.
It is the part of DNA that directly stores energy as triglycerides.
It is covalently linked and forms the structural framework of each strand.
It is formed from amino acids that alternate with phosphate groups.
questions.explain: The backbone provides the strand's continuity, while the bases extend from it and pair across the helix.
choice_explanations: Adjacent sugars and phosphates are covalently linked.
The backbone is not a triglyceride-based energy store.
Correct. The backbone provides the strand's continuity, while the bases extend from it and pair across the helix.
The backbone uses sugars and phosphates, not amino acids.

questions.text: A student says, 'DNA strands are held together by phosphodiester bonds, so heating DNA first breaks those bonds apart.' Which evaluation is best?
choices: The statement is fully incorrect because heating DNA has no effect on any interactions in the molecule.
The statement is fully correct because phosphodiester bonds connect complementary bases across the helix.
The statement is partly correct because phosphodiester bonds are real DNA bonds, but it is incorrect because heating usually separates the strands by disrupting hydrogen bonds between bases first.
The statement is fully incorrect because DNA contains only ionic bonds and no covalent bonds.
questions.explain: This is a classic partially correct distractor: it names a correct bond type but assigns it the wrong structural role in strand separation.
choice_explanations: Heating can disrupt hydrogen bonding and separate the strands.
Phosphodiester bonds are in the backbone, not between paired bases on opposite strands.
Correct. This is a classic partially correct distractor: it names a correct bond type but assigns it the wrong structural role in strand separation.
DNA definitely contains covalent bonds in its backbone.

questions.text: Which feature do nucleic acids and proteins have in common?
choices: Both store energy primarily by forming beta glucose fibers.
Both are polymers built by covalently linking smaller monomer subunits.
Both are made of the same monomer but fold differently.
Both are composed mainly of fatty acids and glycerol.
questions.explain: Although the monomers and bond types differ, both are macromolecules assembled from repeating subunits.
choice_explanations: That describes cellulose-like carbohydrate structure, not proteins or nucleic acids.
Correct. Although the monomers and bond types differ, both are macromolecules assembled from repeating subunits.
Proteins and nucleic acids do not share the same monomer.
That describes lipids, not proteins or nucleic acids.

questions.text: Why can a change in nucleotide sequence matter even before any protein is made?
choices: Because the sequence is part of the nucleic acid's primary information content and can affect future base pairing or coding relationships.
Because nucleotides immediately turn into amino acids as soon as sequence changes occur.
Because any nucleotide change automatically hydrolyzes the entire molecule.
Because nucleotide order matters only for membrane fluidity.
questions.explain: Nucleotide order itself is biologically meaningful, even before translation is considered.
choice_explanations: Correct. Nucleotide order itself is biologically meaningful, even before translation is considered.
Nucleotides and amino acids are different monomers.
A sequence change does not imply whole-molecule hydrolysis.
Sequence information is not primarily a membrane-fluidity issue.

questions.text: A nucleic acid strand is described as 'a chain of repeating sugar-phosphate units with bases attached.' What is the best interpretation?
choices: The molecule must be a protein because it contains repeating subunits.
The chain is a polysaccharide because it contains sugar.
The bases form the backbone, and the sugars merely cross-link them like side chains.
The sugar-phosphate portion forms the backbone, and the attached bases vary to encode information.
questions.explain: This description captures the key architecture of nucleic acids.
choice_explanations: Many macromolecules contain repeating subunits; the described structure is specifically nucleic acid.
The presence of sugar does not make it a carbohydrate polymer when phosphate and bases define the monomer.
The backbone is sugar-phosphate, not base-based.
Correct. This description captures the key architecture of nucleic acids.

questions.text: Which statement best compares phosphodiester bonds and hydrogen bonds in nucleic acids?
choices: Both bond types link amino acids to one another in nucleic acids.
Phosphodiester bonds create the covalent backbone of each strand, whereas hydrogen bonds can link complementary bases between strands.
Hydrogen bonds form the backbone, whereas phosphodiester bonds determine base pairing rules.
Phosphodiester bonds are weaker than hydrogen bonds and therefore break first during strand separation.
questions.explain: The two bond types serve different structural roles in nucleic acid organization.
choice_explanations: Nucleic acids are not polymers of amino acids.
Correct. The two bond types serve different structural roles in nucleic acid organization.
This reverses their roles.
Phosphodiester bonds are strong covalent bonds; hydrogen bonds are weaker.

questions.text: A nucleotide loses its phosphate group. Which description is now most accurate?
choices: It has become an amino acid because phosphate groups define amino acids.
It automatically becomes a triglyceride because phosphate determines lipid class.
It is no longer a complete nucleotide because one of the three defining components is missing.
It remains a full nucleotide because the phosphate is optional in nucleic acids.
questions.explain: A nucleotide requires base, sugar, and phosphate together.
choice_explanations: Amino acids are different monomers with amino and carboxyl groups.
Lipid class is not assigned that way.
Correct. A nucleotide requires base, sugar, and phosphate together.
The phosphate is part of what makes the monomer a nucleotide.

questions.text: Which observation would best support the claim that an unknown polymer is a nucleic acid rather than a carbohydrate?
choices: Hydrolysis yields many glucose molecules with no phosphate or base.
The polymer is composed of amino acids linked by peptide bonds.
Hydrolysis yields monomers that each contain a phosphate group, a pentose sugar, and a nitrogenous base.
The polymer is insoluble in water and made of three fatty acid tails.
questions.explain: That monomer composition identifies nucleotides.
choice_explanations: That would support a carbohydrate polymer instead.
That would indicate a protein.
Correct. That monomer composition identifies nucleotides.
That would point toward a lipid, not a nucleic acid.

questions.text: Why is DNA considered a polymer?
choices: It stores hereditary information and only information-containing molecules are polymers.
It always contains exactly two strands and any two-stranded molecule is a polymer.
It contains many hydrogen bonds and therefore counts as a polymer.
It consists of many nucleotide monomers linked into a long repeating chain.
questions.explain: A polymer is a large molecule built from repeating smaller subunits.
choice_explanations: Information storage is not the defining criterion for a polymer.
Polymer status depends on repeating monomer subunits, not simply on strand number.
Hydrogen bonding alone does not define a polymer.
Correct. A polymer is a large molecule built from repeating smaller subunits.

questions.text: A chemist adds a hydroxyl group (-OH) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: convert the molecule into a protein by creating a peptide backbone
make the molecule more hydrophobic by adding a nonpolar region
increase the molecule's polarity and ability to interact with water
remove all possibility of hydrogen bonding
questions.explain: Increase the molecule's polarity and ability to interact with water.
choice_explanations: Adding one functional group does not convert a carbon skeleton into an entirely different macromolecule class.
A hydroxyl group is polar; it generally does the opposite of making the molecule more hydrophobic.
Correct. Increase the molecule's polarity and ability to interact with water.
Hydroxyl groups actually promote hydrogen bonding rather than eliminating it.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the hydroxyl group (-OH) matters biologically?
choices: It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.
Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.

questions.text: A chemist adds a carboxyl group (-COOH) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: make the molecule permanently nonpolar
turn the molecule into a nucleotide automatically
cause the molecule to lose all carbon atoms during reactions
allow the molecule to act as an acid by donating H+ under appropriate conditions
questions.explain: Allow the molecule to act as an acid by donating H+ under appropriate conditions.
choice_explanations: A carboxyl group is polar/acidic, not a way to make a molecule permanently nonpolar.
A nucleotide requires a sugar, phosphate, and base, not just any carboxyl group.
Functional groups affect reactivity, not the existence of the carbon skeleton itself.
Correct. Allow the molecule to act as an acid by donating H+ under appropriate conditions.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the carboxyl group (-COOH) matters biologically?
choices: It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.
Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.

questions.text: A chemist adds a amino group (-NH2) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: make the molecule an acid because nitrogen always donates H+
remove all charge interactions from the molecule
force the carbon skeleton to form a lipid bilayer
allow the molecule to act as a base by accepting H+ under appropriate conditions
questions.explain: Allow the molecule to act as a base by accepting H+ under appropriate conditions.
choice_explanations: Amino groups tend to accept H+, so this reverses the usual behavior.
Adding an amino group can introduce charge interactions rather than eliminate them.
An amino group alone does not force membrane-bilayer behavior.
Correct. Allow the molecule to act as a base by accepting H+ under appropriate conditions.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the amino group (-NH2) matters biologically?
choices: It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.

questions.text: A chemist adds a phosphate group (-PO4) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: add negative charge and often increase the molecule's ability to interact with water
replace all covalent bonds with ionic bonds
turn the molecule into glycogen
make the molecule less reactive because phosphate is always neutral
questions.explain: Add negative charge and often increase the molecule's ability to interact with water.
choice_explanations: Correct. Add negative charge and often increase the molecule's ability to interact with water.
One functional group does not replace all the molecule's covalent bonds.
Phosphate addition does not convert a molecule into a polysaccharide.
Phosphate groups often contribute negative charge and can increase reactivity in biology.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the phosphate group (-PO4) matters biologically?
choices: It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.

questions.text: A chemist adds a methyl group (-CH3) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: turn the molecule acidic by donating H+ easily
create a peptide bond within the molecule automatically
add strong negative charge that increases solubility
make the region more nonpolar and less able to hydrogen-bond with water
questions.explain: Make the region more nonpolar and less able to hydrogen-bond with water.
choice_explanations: Methyl groups do not act like carboxyl groups in acid donation.
A methyl group does not automatically create a peptide bond.
Methyl groups are nonpolar, not strongly negatively charged.
Correct. Make the region more nonpolar and less able to hydrogen-bond with water.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the methyl group (-CH3) matters biologically?
choices: It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.

questions.text: A chemist adds a sulfhydryl group (-SH) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: function exactly like a phosphate group by adding strong negative charge
always make the molecule highly basic in water
allow potential formation of disulfide bridges that can stabilize protein structure
convert the molecule into cellulose
questions.explain: Allow potential formation of disulfide bridges that can stabilize protein structure.
choice_explanations: Sulfhydryl and phosphate groups have very different chemistry.
Sulfhydryl groups are not defined primarily by making molecules highly basic.
Correct. Allow potential formation of disulfide bridges that can stabilize protein structure.
Adding sulfur does not make a protein into cellulose.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the sulfhydryl group (-SH) matters biologically?
choices: It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.
Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.

questions.text: A chemist adds a carbonyl group (C=O) to a carbon skeleton. What is the most likely effect on the molecule's behavior?
choices: increase polarity in that part of the molecule and help distinguish aldehydes from ketones
make the molecule a saturated fat automatically
remove oxygen from the molecule entirely
force the molecule to become an ion with full positive charge
questions.explain: Increase polarity in that part of the molecule and help distinguish aldehydes from ketones.
choice_explanations: Correct. Increase polarity in that part of the molecule and help distinguish aldehydes from ketones.
A carbonyl group does not turn a molecule into a fat.
A carbonyl group includes oxygen; it does not remove it.
Carbonyl groups increase polarity but do not inherently give a full positive charge to the whole molecule.

questions.text: Which statement best captures why the carbonyl group (C=O) matters biologically?
choices: It changes only the number of carbon atoms and has no effect on polarity or reactivity.
It changes the molecule's chemical properties even if the carbon skeleton stays the same.
It forces every molecule containing it to serve the same cellular function.
It matters only in inorganic minerals and never in organic molecules.
questions.explain: Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
choice_explanations: Functional groups often affect polarity, charge, and reactivity without changing the carbon count.
Correct. Functional groups influence how molecules behave chemically; they do not have to replace the underlying carbon skeleton.
The same functional group can appear in molecules with different functions.
Functional groups are crucial in many organic biological molecules.

questions.text: Two molecules have the same carbon skeleton, but one contains a hydroxyl group and the other contains a methyl group. Which statement is most likely true?
choices: Both molecules must have identical solubility because carbon skeleton determines everything.
The hydroxyl-containing molecule is likely to be more hydrophilic because hydroxyl groups are polar whereas methyl groups are nonpolar.
The hydroxyl-containing molecule becomes ionic in all cases and therefore is no longer an organic molecule.
The methyl-containing molecule is more hydrophilic because methyl groups form more hydrogen bonds with water.
questions.explain: A polar functional group such as hydroxyl often increases hydrophilicity compared with a nonpolar methyl group.
choice_explanations: Functional groups can strongly affect solubility even when carbon skeletons are similar.
Correct. A polar functional group such as hydroxyl often increases hydrophilicity compared with a nonpolar methyl group.
A molecule with a hydroxyl group can still be an organic molecule; adding a hydroxyl group does not automatically make it ionic.
Methyl groups are nonpolar and do not generally increase hydrogen bonding with water.

questions.text: Why does oxygen usually carry a partial negative charge in a water molecule?
choices: Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen and pulls the shared electrons closer to itself.
Oxygen steals hydrogen's electrons completely, creating ionic bonds in water.
Oxygen is negatively charged as an isolated atom, so every bond it forms stays fully negative.
Oxygen has fewer protons than hydrogen and therefore attracts electrons more strongly.
questions.explain: Unequal electron sharing creates partial charges and makes water polar.
choice_explanations: Correct. Unequal electron sharing creates partial charges and makes water polar.
Water has polar covalent bonds, not fully ionic bonds.
Partial negative charge in water arises from unequal sharing within a covalent bond, not from oxygen being a permanently negative atom.
Hydrogen has fewer protons; the relevant idea is electronegativity, not that incorrect proton comparison.

questions.text: A bond between two atoms with very different electronegativities is most likely to be
choices: nonpolar covalent because unequal attraction always cancels out.
a peptide bond because electronegativity determines polymer type.
a hydrogen bond inside a single atom.
polar covalent or ionic, depending on how unequal the electron sharing becomes.
questions.explain: As electronegativity difference increases, sharing becomes increasingly unequal and may approach full transfer.
choice_explanations: Nonpolar covalent bonding requires roughly equal electron sharing, not very different electronegativities.
Peptide bonds are a specific covalent linkage between amino acids.
Hydrogen bonds are intermolecular or between parts of molecules, not 'inside a single atom'.
Correct. As electronegativity difference increases, sharing becomes increasingly unequal and may approach full transfer.

questions.text: How does an isotope of carbon differ from another isotope of carbon?
choices: It has the same number of neutrons but a different number of chemical bonds.
It has a different number of protons and therefore becomes a different isotope of the same element.
It has a different number of valence electrons but the same number of neutrons.
It has a different number of neutrons but the same number of protons.
questions.explain: The proton number defines the element; neutron number can vary among isotopes.
choice_explanations: Bond number is not what defines isotopes.
Changing proton number changes the element itself, not just the isotope.
That would describe an ion or excited state issue, not an isotope definition.
Correct. The proton number defines the element; neutron number can vary among isotopes.

questions.text: Why can radioactive isotopes be useful in biological research?
choices: They change one element into a completely different element instantly when placed in water.
They create hydrogen bonds that ordinary isotopes cannot form.
They can serve as tracers, allowing scientists to follow the movement of atoms through molecules and organisms.
They make all molecules containing them nonpolar.
questions.explain: Because the isotope can be detected, it helps researchers track where a particular element goes.
choice_explanations: Tracer use does not rely on instant element conversion in water.
Chemical bonding behavior is usually very similar among isotopes of the same element.
Correct. Because the isotope can be detected, it helps researchers track where a particular element goes.
An isotope does not automatically determine polarity.

questions.text: Which statement best describes a covalent bond?
choices: It is the weak attraction that occurs between neighboring water molecules.
It forms when atoms exchange neutrons.
It forms only between ions after complete electron transfer.
It forms when atoms share electrons.
questions.explain: Covalent bonds are built on shared electron pairs.
choice_explanations: That describes hydrogen bonding, not a covalent bond.
Neutrons are not exchanged to form ordinary chemical bonds.
That description fits ionic bonding more closely.
Correct. Covalent bonds are built on shared electron pairs.

questions.text: What usually happens when an atom becomes an ion?
choices: It gains or loses protons and remains the same element.
It gains or loses one or more electrons and acquires a net charge.
It forms a hydrogen bond with another atom.
It changes the number of neutrons and becomes electrically charged for that reason.
questions.explain: Ions form when electron number no longer balances proton number.
choice_explanations: Changing proton number would change the element itself.
Correct. Ions form when electron number no longer balances proton number.
Becoming an ion is not the same as forming a hydrogen bond.
Charge depends on electrons and protons, not neutrons.

questions.text: A molecule with equal electron sharing and no net partial charges is best described as
choices: acidic.
ionic.
nonpolar.
radioactive.
questions.explain: Without a significant charge separation, the molecule is nonpolar.
choice_explanations: Acidity refers to hydrogen ion behavior in solution, not simply to electron sharing.
Ionic substances involve charge separation through electron transfer, not equal sharing.
Correct. Without a significant charge separation, the molecule is nonpolar.
Radioactivity concerns nuclear instability, not charge distribution.

questions.text: Why can carbon form a wide variety of organic molecules?
choices: It has no valence electrons and therefore can attach to any atom without limit.
It is the only element that can bond to hydrogen.
Its four valence electrons allow it to form up to four covalent bonds in many arrangements.
It forms only ionic bonds, which are more flexible than covalent bonds.
questions.explain: This bonding flexibility lets carbon make chains, rings, and branched skeletons.
choice_explanations: Carbon does have valence electrons; four is a central reason for its versatility.
Many elements can bond to hydrogen.
Correct. This bonding flexibility lets carbon make chains, rings, and branched skeletons.
Carbon commonly forms covalent, not mainly ionic, bonds in organic molecules.

questions.text: Which statement correctly compares ionic and covalent bonding?
choices: Covalent bonds form only between metals, whereas ionic bonds form only between nonmetals.
Ionic and covalent bonds both rely on identical equal sharing of electrons.
Ionic bonding involves electrostatic attraction between ions formed after electron transfer, whereas covalent bonding involves shared electrons.
Covalent bonds are always weaker than hydrogen bonds.
questions.explain: The two bond types differ mainly in how electrons are handled.
choice_explanations: That oversimplifies incorrectly. Ionic bonds often involve metal and nonmetal, while covalent bonds commonly involve nonmetals.
Equal sharing is associated with nonpolar covalent bonding, not ionic bonding.
Correct. The two bond types differ mainly in how electrons are handled.
Hydrogen bonds are generally weaker than covalent bonds.

questions.text: A student says, 'Hydrogen bonds in water are strong because they involve complete electron transfer.' What is the best evaluation?
choices: The statement is partly correct because hydrogen bonds do contribute important stability in water, but it is incorrect because they do not involve complete electron transfer.
The statement is fully incorrect because electron transfer occurs only in proteins.
The statement is fully correct because hydrogen bonds are a type of ionic bond.
The statement is fully incorrect because water molecules do not attract one another at all.
questions.explain: Hydrogen bonds are weaker attractions between partial charges, not full ionic bonds.
choice_explanations: Correct. Hydrogen bonds are weaker attractions between partial charges, not full ionic bonds.
Electron transfer can occur in many chemical contexts, but not in hydrogen bonds of water.
Hydrogen bonds are not full ionic bonds.
Water molecules do attract one another strongly enough to produce important bulk properties.

questions.text: What determines whether two atoms form a polar covalent bond instead of a nonpolar covalent bond?
choices: Whether the atoms are both found in living organisms.
The temperature of the room in which the atoms collide.
The degree of difference in their electronegativities.
Whether the atoms contain the same number of neutrons.
questions.explain: Greater electronegativity difference leads to more unequal electron sharing and thus more polarity.
choice_explanations: Biological occurrence does not define the bond type.
Temperature can affect reaction rates or states but does not define bond polarity itself.
Correct. Greater electronegativity difference leads to more unequal electron sharing and thus more polarity.
Neutron number does not directly determine bond polarity.

questions.text: Why are isotopes of the same element chemically similar in most reactions?
choices: They always have the same mass, so reactions cannot distinguish them.
They have the same number of protons and usually the same electron arrangement, so they behave similarly in bonding.
They have identical radioactivity in all cases, which forces similar chemistry.
They have the same number of neutrons, which determines chemical reactivity.
questions.explain: Chemistry is driven mainly by electron structure, which isotopes of the same element largely share.
choice_explanations: Isotopes differ in mass.
Correct. Chemistry is driven mainly by electron structure, which isotopes of the same element largely share.
Radioactivity is not universal across isotopes and is not the main reason for similar chemical behavior.
Isotopes do not necessarily have the same number of neutrons; that is what differs.

questions.text: A molecule contains both polar covalent bonds and an overall unequal distribution of charge. Which description is most accurate?
choices: The molecule must be radioactive because charge is unequal.
The molecule must be a polymer because it has more than one bond type.
The molecule must be ionic because any polar bond creates full charges.
The molecule is polar.
questions.explain: Polar bonds plus an asymmetrical arrangement can produce an overall dipole.
choice_explanations: Unequal charge distribution is not the same as radioactivity.
Polymer status depends on repeating monomers, not simply on bond polarity.
Polar bonds involve partial charges, not necessarily full ionic charges.
Correct. Polar bonds plus an asymmetrical arrangement can produce an overall dipole.

questions.text: Which subatomic particle is most directly responsible for an atom's chemical bonding behavior?
choices: Its nucleus as a whole, because bonds form when nuclei combine.
Its protons, because they are the particles that are shared in covalent bonds.
Its neutrons, because they determine the atom's charge.
Its electrons, especially the valence electrons.
questions.explain: Bonding depends on how electrons are arranged and shared or transferred.
choice_explanations: Chemical bonds involve electron interactions, not fusion of nuclei.
Protons are not shared in covalent bonding.
Neutrons are neutral and do not determine charge.
Correct. Bonding depends on how electrons are arranged and shared or transferred.

questions.text: Which change would turn one neutral atom into a cation?
choices: Gain of one or more protons without changing anything else, while remaining the same element.
Loss of one or more neutrons.
Loss of one or more electrons.
Gain of one or more electrons.
questions.explain: Removing negatively charged electrons leaves the atom with a net positive charge.
choice_explanations: Changing proton number changes the element and is not the ordinary description of ion formation.
Changing neutron number makes an isotope, not a cation.
Correct. Removing negatively charged electrons leaves the atom with a net positive charge.
That would make the atom more negative, producing an anion.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule contains glycerol and three fatty acids and is mainly used for long-term energy storage. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a polysaccharide
a nucleotide
a phospholipid
a triglyceride
questions.explain: The description matches a triglyceride.
choice_explanations: A polysaccharide is a carbohydrate polymer of sugar monomers.
A nucleotide is the monomer of nucleic acids and has sugar, phosphate, and base.
A phospholipid has a phosphate-containing head and usually two fatty acid tails rather than three.
Correct. The description matches a triglyceride.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is a polymer of amino acids linked by peptide bonds. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a nucleic acid
a protein
a triglyceride
a carbohydrate
questions.explain: The description matches a protein.
choice_explanations: Nucleic acids are built from nucleotides and use phosphodiester bonds in the backbone.
Correct. The description matches a protein.
Triglycerides are lipids, not amino acid polymers.
Carbohydrates are built from sugar monomers and use glycosidic bonds.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is a polymer of nucleotides that stores or transmits genetic information. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a steroid
a protein
a nucleic acid
a disaccharide
questions.explain: The description matches a nucleic acid.
choice_explanations: Steroids are ring-based lipids, not nucleotide polymers.
Proteins are amino acid polymers, not nucleotide polymers.
Correct. The description matches a nucleic acid.
A disaccharide is just two sugars linked together.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is made of many glucose monomers and serves as a plant storage molecule. What is it most likely to be?
choices: cellulose
starch
chitin
glycogen
questions.explain: The description matches starch.
choice_explanations: Cellulose is structural in plant cell walls.
Correct. The description matches starch.
Chitin is structural in fungi and arthropods.
Glycogen is the main animal storage polysaccharide.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule forms the main structural framework of plant cell walls. What is it most likely to be?
choices: starch
triglyceride
glycogen
cellulose
questions.explain: The description matches cellulose.
choice_explanations: Starch is plant storage carbohydrate, not plant wall structure.
Triglycerides are lipids used mainly for energy storage.
Glycogen is animal storage carbohydrate.
Correct. The description matches cellulose.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule contains a phosphate group, a five-carbon sugar, and a nitrogenous base. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a nucleotide
a monosaccharide
an amino acid
a phospholipid
questions.explain: The description matches a nucleotide.
choice_explanations: Correct. The description matches a nucleotide.
A monosaccharide is a simple sugar lacking the base-and-phosphate combination.
An amino acid has amino, carboxyl, and R groups rather than sugar-phosphate-base.
A phospholipid is a membrane lipid, not a nucleic acid monomer.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule has four fused carbon rings and may function as a hormone. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a phospholipid
a steroid
a polysaccharide
a triglyceride
questions.explain: The description matches a steroid.
choice_explanations: Phospholipids have hydrophilic heads and fatty acid tails, not the classic steroid ring pattern.
Correct. The description matches a steroid.
Polysaccharides are sugar polymers, not ring-based lipids.
Triglycerides use glycerol and fatty acids, not a four-ring skeleton.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule has a hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tails and is a major membrane component. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a protein
a triglyceride
glycogen
a phospholipid
questions.explain: The description matches a phospholipid.
choice_explanations: Proteins can be membrane components too, but the head-and-tail amphipathic molecule described is a phospholipid.
Triglycerides are storage lipids and lack the same amphipathic membrane-forming structure.
Glycogen is a carbohydrate polymer.
Correct. The description matches a phospholipid.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is a single sugar unit such as glucose or fructose. What is it most likely to be?
choices: an amino acid
a monosaccharide
a polysaccharide
a disaccharide
questions.explain: The description matches a monosaccharide.
choice_explanations: An amino acid is a protein monomer, not a sugar unit.
Correct. The description matches a monosaccharide.
A polysaccharide contains many sugar monomers.
A disaccharide contains two sugar units joined together.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is made of two monosaccharides joined together. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a steroid
a polysaccharide
a disaccharide
a monosaccharide
questions.explain: The description matches a disaccharide.
choice_explanations: A steroid is a lipid, not a carbohydrate of two sugars.
A polysaccharide is many linked sugar monomers.
Correct. The description matches a disaccharide.
A monosaccharide is only one sugar unit.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule contains an amino group, a carboxyl group, and a variable side chain. What is it most likely to be?
choices: a fatty acid
a nucleotide
a monosaccharide
an amino acid
questions.explain: The description matches an amino acid.
choice_explanations: A fatty acid is mainly a hydrocarbon chain with a carboxyl group but not the amino group and R-group arrangement of an amino acid.
A nucleotide includes sugar, phosphate, and base.
A monosaccharide lacks the amino-group/carboxyl-group pattern.
Correct. The description matches an amino acid.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule serves as the highly branched glucose storage polymer in animals. What is it most likely to be?
choices: chitin
cellulose
starch
glycogen
questions.explain: The description matches glycogen.
choice_explanations: Chitin is structural in fungi and arthropods.
Cellulose is structural in plants.
Starch is plant storage carbohydrate.
Correct. The description matches glycogen.

questions.text: An unknown biological molecule is the structural polysaccharide found in fungal cell walls. What is it most likely to be?
choices: cellulose
DNA
glycogen
chitin
questions.explain: The description matches chitin.
choice_explanations: Cellulose is the main structural polysaccharide of plant cell walls, not fungal walls.
DNA is a nucleic acid, not a structural polysaccharide.
Glycogen is storage carbohydrate in animals.
Correct. The description matches chitin.

a solvent
a buffer
a polymer

questions.text: An enzyme is heated well above its normal temperature and then loses activity. Which explanation is best?

denaturation affects only the temperature of the solution and not the shape of the protein
the protein can lose function only if every peptide bond is hydrolyzed into separate amino acids
heating can disrupt interactions that maintain protein shape, altering the active site and reducing function

only nucleic acids can be affected by environmental conditions in this way

questions.text: Which explanation best accounts for the observation that sweat evaporates from the skin?

questions.text: Which explanation best accounts for the observation that water beads on a waxy leaf surface?

questions.text: Which statement correctly compares a weak acid and its conjugate base?

questions.explain: A narrower tube increases the relative effect of adhesion and cohesion on the water column.
choice_explanations: Tube diameter matters to capillary rise even if the material is the same.
Capillary action can oppose gravity over short distances in narrow spaces.
Holding more water does not mean a higher capillary rise.
Correct. A narrower tube increases the relative effect of adhesion and cohesion on the water column.

questions.text: Three narrow glass tubes of different diameters are placed upright in a dish of colored water. The height of the water column in each tube is recorded. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The water will rise highest in the narrowest tube because capillary action is stronger there.
The water will rise equally in all tubes because glass composition is the same.
The widest tube will rise highest because it holds the greatest mass of water.
No tube will show capillary rise because gravity always prevents upward movement in liquids.
questions.explain: A narrower tube increases the relative effect of adhesion and cohesion on the water column.
choice_explanations: Correct. A narrower tube increases the relative effect of adhesion and cohesion on the water column.

questions.text: A botanist compares water movement up strips of cellulose paper and strips coated with wax. Both are placed in beakers of water. Which result is most likely?
choices: Both strips will move water equally because cohesion alone determines all capillary effects.
The wax-coated strips will move water higher because nonpolar surfaces attract water more strongly.
The cellulose strips will show greater upward movement because water adheres better to the polar cellulose surface.
Neither strip will move water because water molecules cannot attract each other in narrow channels.
questions.explain: The wax coating reduces favorable interaction between water and the strip surface, weakening capillary movement.
choice_explanations: Cohesion matters, but the surface interaction difference shows that adhesion also matters.
Nonpolar wax does not attract polar water more strongly than cellulose does.
Correct. The wax coating reduces favorable interaction between water and the strip surface, weakening capillary movement.
Water molecules do attract each other; cohesion is part of the effect.

questions.text: A researcher places one end of a celery stalk in red dye solution and tracks how quickly the dye rises through the stem. Which result is most likely?
choices: The dye rises as water moves through xylem by cohesion and adhesion.
The dye rises because xylem cells pump each water molecule upward with ATP at every step.
The dye rises only because the dye molecules are nonpolar.
The dye rises because covalent bonds form permanently between neighboring water molecules in xylem.
questions.explain: The colored solution is carried upward because water sticks to xylem walls and to other water molecules.
choice_explanations: Correct. The colored solution is carried upward because water sticks to xylem walls and to other water molecules.
The scenario is explained mainly by bulk water movement properties, not ATP-driven pumping of each individual molecule through xylem.
Nonpolarity of the dye is not the main reason the solution rises.
Water molecules interact by hydrogen bonding, not by forming permanent covalent chains in xylem.

questions.text: Identical strips of filter paper are placed into pure water and salt water. A student measures how far the liquid fronts rise in a fixed time interval. Which result is most likely?
choices: The paper pulls liquid upward by breaking water's covalent bonds.
Capillary action depends only on the color of the liquid, not on molecular interactions.
The liquid front rises only if the solution is neutral at pH 7.
Capillary rise still depends on water's adhesive and cohesive properties, although solutes may alter the exact rate.
questions.explain: The experiment tests intermolecular interactions between water and the paper as well as water-water attraction.
choice_explanations: The paper does not break the covalent bonds within water molecules.
Capillary action is a molecular interaction phenomenon, not a color phenomenon.
Neutral pH is not the requirement for capillary action here.
Correct. The experiment tests intermolecular interactions between water and the paper as well as water-water attraction.

questions.text: A student slowly adds water droplets to the top of a penny and counts how many fit before the water spills over the edge. Which result is most likely?
choices: The droplets pile up only because the penny is made of metal, and metals create pH buffers.
The droplets pile up because water molecules form permanent covalent bonds with the penny.
Many droplets can accumulate because cohesion creates surface tension at the water surface.
The droplets pile up because water has no intermolecular attractions at its surface.
questions.explain: Hydrogen bonding among water molecules helps the drop hold together in a dome before spilling.
choice_explanations: The coin's material can matter somewhat, but the main reason the dome forms is surface tension, not pH buffering.
Water does not form permanent covalent bonds with the penny in this demonstration.
Correct. Hydrogen bonding among water molecules helps the drop hold together in a dome before spilling.
Surface tension exists precisely because water molecules do have intermolecular attractions.

questions.text: A biology class places a clean paper clip carefully on the surface of water and observes that it can remain supported temporarily. Which result is most likely?
choices: The clip floats because water molecules underneath it become ionic.
The clip floats because iron always has lower density than water.
The clip is supported because adhesion to air is stronger than gravity.
The clip is supported by surface tension created by cohesive forces among water molecules at the surface.
questions.explain: The surface resists being broken because the water molecules there are strongly attracted to one another.
choice_explanations: The water molecules do not become ionic in order to support the clip.
A metal paper clip is denser than water; density alone does not explain this observation.
The key force is cohesion within the water surface, not adhesion to air.
Correct. The surface resists being broken because the water molecules there are strongly attracted to one another.

questions.text: One beaker contains pure water and another contains water mixed with detergent. A paper clip is placed carefully on each surface. Which result is most likely?
choices: The detergent solution supports the clip better because surfactants convert water into oil.
The paper clip is more likely to stay up on the detergent solution because detergents create stronger hydrogen bonds in water.
The paper clip is less likely to remain supported on the detergent solution because detergent disrupts surface tension.
Both beakers should behave identically because detergents affect only color, not intermolecular forces.
questions.explain: Interfering with cohesion at the surface reduces the ability of the liquid surface to resist breaking.
choice_explanations: Surfactants do not convert water into oil.
Detergents tend to reduce surface tension rather than increase it.
Correct. Interfering with cohesion at the surface reduces the ability of the liquid surface to resist breaking.
Detergents can strongly affect intermolecular behavior at surfaces.
