questions.text: A student compares drop shape on a waxed leaf and on a clean glass slide. The drop on the waxed leaf is rounder and taller. Which result is most likely?
choices: The rounder drop forms because wax increases the pH of the water.
The rounder drop reflects stronger cohesion relative to adhesion on the waxy surface.
The drop is rounder because water loses hydrogen bonding on a leaf.
The rounder drop means adhesion to wax is stronger than cohesion.
questions.explain: On the nonpolar wax, water interacts more strongly with itself than with the surface, preserving a rounded shape.
choice_explanations: pH is not the central explanation for the droplet shape here.
Correct. On the nonpolar wax, water interacts more strongly with itself than with the surface, preserving a rounded shape.
Hydrogen bonding among water molecules still contributes to the rounded droplet.
If adhesion were stronger, the droplet would spread more, not remain rounded.

questions.text: A researcher photographs small insects standing on the surface of a pond before and after adding a small amount of soap. Which result is most likely?
choices: After soap is added, the insects are more stable because soap makes water more polar.
Soap has no effect because surface tension depends only on water temperature.
The insects sink because the soap makes the pond acidic enough to dissolve them.
After soap is added, the insects are more likely to break through the surface because surface tension decreases.
questions.explain: Soap reduces cohesion at the surface, weakening the support the insects previously relied on.
choice_explanations: Soap does not support them by making water 'more polar' in the relevant sense; it lowers surface tension.
Surface tension depends on intermolecular forces, not only on temperature.
The main observed effect is loss of support from reduced surface tension, not acid dissolution.
Correct. Soap reduces cohesion at the surface, weakening the support the insects previously relied on.

questions.text: A student prepares two beakers: one with distilled water and one with a phosphate buffer, both at pH 7. Equal drops of HCl are added to each beaker. Which result is most likely?
choices: The buffered beaker will show the smaller drop in pH.
The buffered beaker will show the larger pH drop because buffers release extra H+ whenever acid is added.
Both beakers will show exactly the same pH change because the same number of acid drops was added.
The distilled-water beaker will resist change better because it has fewer dissolved particles.
questions.explain: The buffer can bind some of the added H+, so the change in free hydrogen ion concentration is smaller than in distilled water.
choice_explanations: Correct. The buffer can bind some of the added H+, so the change in free hydrogen ion concentration is smaller than in distilled water.
Buffers do not magnify acid effects by releasing extra H+ in this context; they reduce the pH shift.
Equal acid addition can yield different pH changes when the starting solutions have different chemistry.
Distilled water lacks a conjugate acid-base pair and therefore buffers poorly.

questions.text: A teacher adds a fixed amount of acid to three solutions: pure water, a bicarbonate buffer, and a weak acid/conjugate base mixture. pH is measured before and after. Which result is most likely?
choices: The solutions containing buffer components should show smaller pH changes than pure water.
Pure water should change the least because it has no extra molecules to interfere.
All three must reach the same final pH because the same acid was added.
Buffered solutions become basic after acid is added because bases are present.
questions.explain: A buffer reduces the size of the pH shift by accepting some of the disturbance.
choice_explanations: Correct. A buffer reduces the size of the pH shift by accepting some of the disturbance.
Pure water is not the best pH stabilizer here.
Final pH depends on the composition of each solution, not just the amount of acid added.
Buffers do not necessarily overshoot to become basic when acid is added.

questions.text: A lab group graphs pH over time as acid is added dropwise to a beaker of water and to a beaker of buffer. Which result is most likely?
choices: The buffer graph should decline more gradually at first.
The buffer graph should fall more steeply because buffers are more chemically active.
The two graphs should overlap perfectly because pH is measured on the same scale.
The water graph should stay flatter because water molecules already contain hydrogen.
questions.explain: Initial resistance to pH change is a hallmark of buffering capacity.
choice_explanations: Correct. Initial resistance to pH change is a hallmark of buffering capacity.
Chemical activity does not mean a steeper pH change; buffers are supposed to moderate it.
Sharing a measurement scale does not mean the behavior is identical.
The presence of hydrogen atoms in water does not make pure water an effective buffer.

questions.text: A researcher tests blood plasma and saline solution by adding equal small amounts of acid to each and measuring pH immediately after. Which result is most likely?
choices: Blood plasma should show the smaller immediate pH change because it contains buffering systems.
Plasma should change more because proteins make the solution unstable.
Saline should buffer better because dissolved salt always acts as a buffer.
Both should react the same because both are liquids.
questions.explain: Biological fluids often contain conjugate acid-base pairs that help maintain pH homeostasis.
choice_explanations: Correct. Biological fluids often contain conjugate acid-base pairs that help maintain pH homeostasis.
Proteins and other components can contribute to buffering rather than making pH inherently unstable.
Ordinary dissolved salt is not automatically a pH buffer.
Being liquids does not make their acid-response identical.

choices: Both should react the same because both are liquids.
Plasma should change more because proteins make the solution unstable.

questions.text: A class cools equal masses of two fats. Fat A is rich in saturated fatty acids, and Fat B is rich in unsaturated fatty acids. Students record whether each fat is solid or liquid at room temperature. Which result is most likely?

Fat A is more likely to be liquid because straight tails cannot interact closely.
questions.explain: Saturated tails are straighter and pack more tightly, which tends to raise the melting point.
choice_explanations: Double bonds usually introduce kinks that reduce packing rather than strengthen it.
Glycerol is shared, but fatty acid saturation strongly affects physical behavior.
Correct. Saturated tails are straighter and pack more tightly, which tends to raise the melting point.
Straight tails do interact closely; that is why saturated fats often solidify more readily.

Sample Y should become a protein in the cold because of the double bonds.
questions.explain: Unsaturated tails prevent tight packing, helping membranes stay fluid at lower temperatures.

questions.text: A scientist synthesizes two fats with the same number of carbons but different tail geometry. One has kinked tails; the other has straight tails. Packing is compared. Which result is most likely?

questions.text: A researcher shakes phospholipids into a beaker of water and then examines the mixture under a microscope. Which result is most likely?
choices: The phospholipids will orient with tails facing outward toward the water and heads buried inside.
The phospholipids will dissolve completely as single molecules because their tails form hydrogen bonds with water.
The phospholipids will behave like glucose monomers and form polysaccharide chains.
Many phospholipids will arrange so that hydrophilic heads face water and hydrophobic tails cluster away from it.
questions.explain: Because phospholipids are amphipathic, water drives them into organized structures such as bilayers or vesicles.
choice_explanations: In water, the hydrophilic heads face outward and the hydrophobic tails are sheltered inward.
Hydrophobic tails do not interact favorably with water in that way.
Phospholipids do not become carbohydrate polymers in water.
Correct. Because phospholipids are amphipathic, water drives them into organized structures such as bilayers or vesicles.

questions.text: A class compares a beaker containing phospholipids in water with a beaker containing triglycerides in water. The mixtures are stirred and then left to settle. Which result is most likely?
choices: Neither can organize in water because all lipids are completely insoluble in any aqueous environment.

questions.text: A student adds a dye that dissolves only in water to a suspension of phospholipid vesicles and observes that the dye remains outside the vesicle interior unless the vesicle is broken. Which result is most likely?
choices: The dye stays outside because phospholipids become covalently linked into a solid polymer shell.
The dye stays outside because vesicles are made of cellulose walls.
The dye stays outside because phospholipids eliminate all water from the vesicle interior.
The vesicle has an aqueous interior separated by a phospholipid bilayer barrier.
questions.explain: Self-assembled bilayers can enclose water, creating compartments with inside and outside environments.
choice_explanations: Bilayer assembly is driven by intermolecular organization, not polymerization into a solid shell.
A phospholipid vesicle is not a cellulose wall structure.
Vesicles usually enclose water; they do not eliminate it from the interior.
Correct. Self-assembled bilayers can enclose water, creating compartments with inside and outside environments.

questions.text: A lab removes the phosphate heads from a sample of phospholipids and then places the altered molecules in water. Which result is most likely?

questions.text: A student places starch solution in Tube A and cellulose suspension in Tube B. Amylase is added to both tubes, and the amount of glucose released is measured over time. Which result is most likely?
choices: Neither tube should release glucose because polymers cannot be hydrolyzed enzymatically.
Tube B should show more glucose because cellulose contains more glucose than starch.

Total glucose content is not the main issue; enzyme access to the correct bond type is.

Same monomer does not guarantee the same susceptibility to a given enzyme.

questions.text: Two enzyme mixtures are compared: one contains cellulase and one contains amylase. Each is added separately to starch and cellulose samples. Which result is most likely?

Cellulase should work better on starch because starch is a plant polymer.

questions.text: A lab hydrolyzes an unknown glucose polymer with a human digestive enzyme and sees almost no monomer release. With a fungal enzyme, monomer release is high. Which result is most likely?
choices: The unknown polymer is likely glycogen because glycogen is always indigestible to humans.
The polymer must be DNA because fungal enzymes digest all nucleic acids.
The unknown polymer is probably a lipid because lipids also release little glucose.
The unknown polymer is likely cellulose or another beta-linked structural carbohydrate rather than starch.
questions.explain: Humans digest alpha-linked starch well, but many cannot hydrolyze beta linkages in cellulose without specialized enzymes.
choice_explanations: Humans can digest glycogen-derived glucose just fine; the key clue is failure to digest a beta-linked carbohydrate.

A glucose polymer is not a lipid.
Correct. Humans digest alpha-linked starch well, but many cannot hydrolyze beta linkages in cellulose without specialized enzymes.

Blocking a functional group does not remove amino acids from the sequence.

questions.text: Two molecules have the same carbon skeleton, but one contains a carboxyl group and the other does not. Their behavior in an aqueous solution is compared. Which result is most likely?
choices: The carboxyl group turns the molecule into a nucleotide.
The carboxyl group matters only in proteins and never in small organic molecules.
The carboxyl-containing molecule must be more nonpolar because extra oxygen reduces solubility.
The molecule with the carboxyl group is more likely to show acidic behavior under appropriate conditions.
questions.explain: Carboxyl groups can donate H+, affecting charge and reactivity in water.
choice_explanations: A nucleotide requires more than simply a carboxyl group.
Functional groups matter in many kinds of organic molecules, not only proteins.
Carboxyl groups are polar and often increase hydrophilicity rather than reducing it.
Correct. Carboxyl groups can donate H+, affecting charge and reactivity in water.

questions.text: A lab adds a phosphate group to an organic molecule and then measures its movement in water relative to the unmodified version. Which result is most likely?
choices: The phosphorylated molecule becomes more hydrophobic because phosphate removes all polarity.
The phosphate group converts the molecule into a saturated fat.
The phosphate group matters only if the molecule is DNA.
The phosphorylated molecule often becomes more polar and may interact more strongly with water.
questions.explain: Phosphate groups can contribute negative charge and alter molecular interactions substantially.

Correct. Phosphate groups can contribute negative charge and alter molecular interactions substantially.

questions.text: Two proteins differ by one side chain: one has a sulfhydryl-containing residue, and the other lacks it. Folding behavior is compared. Which result is most likely?

questions.text: An unknown molecule is hydrolyzed, yielding many amino acids. The intact molecule's shape is altered by heat. Which result is most likely?
choices: The original molecule was most likely a polysaccharide.
The original molecule was most likely a protein.
The original molecule was most likely a triglyceride.
The original molecule was most likely pure water.
questions.explain: Amino acid monomers and heat-sensitive folding are strong clues for protein identity.
choice_explanations: Polysaccharides hydrolyze into sugar monomers, not amino acids.
Correct. Amino acid monomers and heat-sensitive folding are strong clues for protein identity.
Triglycerides break into glycerol and fatty acids, not amino acids.
Water is not hydrolyzed into amino acids.

questions.text: Hydrolysis of an unknown polymer produces monomers that each contain a phosphate group, a pentose sugar, and a nitrogenous base. Which result is most likely?
choices: The polymer was most likely a protein.
The polymer was most likely glycogen.
The polymer was most likely a nucleic acid.
The polymer was most likely a saturated fat.
questions.explain: Those monomers are nucleotides, which identify the polymer class.
choice_explanations: Proteins hydrolyze into amino acids.
Glycogen hydrolyzes into glucose monomers.
Correct. Those monomers are nucleotides, which identify the polymer class.
A saturated fat is a lipid, not a nucleotide polymer.

questions.text: An unknown molecule has glycerol and three fatty acids and is insoluble in water. It is used by the organism for long-term energy storage. Which result is most likely?
choices: The molecule is most likely RNA.
The molecule is most likely a phospholipid.
The molecule is most likely cellulose.
The molecule is most likely a triglyceride.
questions.explain: That composition and function match a storage fat.
choice_explanations: RNA is a nucleic acid, not a storage fat.
A phospholipid usually has two fatty acid tails and a phosphate-containing head.
Cellulose is a glucose polymer, not glycerol plus fatty acids.
Correct. That composition and function match a storage fat.

questions.text: An unknown plant polymer yields only glucose monomers after hydrolysis and forms strong fibers in the cell wall. Which result is most likely?
choices: The polymer is most likely starch because all plant glucose polymers are storage molecules.
The polymer is most likely glycogen because branching creates strong fibers.
The polymer is most likely a steroid because it is found in plants.
The polymer is most likely cellulose.
questions.explain: A structural glucose polymer in plant cell walls is cellulose.
choice_explanations: Not all plant glucose polymers are for storage; cellulose is structural.
Glycogen is animal storage carbohydrate, not plant cell-wall fiber.
A steroid is a lipid with fused rings, not a glucose fiber polymer.
Correct. A structural glucose polymer in plant cell walls is cellulose.

questions.text: A membrane-associated molecule has a phosphate-containing head and two fatty acid tails. In water, it forms bilayer-like structures. Which result is most likely?
choices: The molecule is most likely a triglyceride because all lipids form bilayers.
The molecule is most likely a phospholipid.
The molecule is most likely a nucleotide because it contains a phosphate group.
The molecule is most likely glycogen because it is found in cells.
questions.explain: Its amphipathic structure explains both its composition and its behavior in water.
choice_explanations: Not all lipids form bilayers; triglycerides lack the same amphipathic design.
Correct. Its amphipathic structure explains both its composition and its behavior in water.
A phosphate group alone does not make something a nucleotide.
Glycogen is a carbohydrate polymer, not a membrane-forming amphipathic molecule.
